quick_facts.label: Core remit
quick_facts.value: Coordinates apparel supplier and factory selection, fabric and trim development, commercial terms, sampling, quality, and delivery readiness.

quick_facts.label: Relevant preparation
quick_facts.value: B.F.Tech (Apparel Production) covers raw materials including fabrics and trims, production planning, merchandising, work study, and quality control—knowledge areas directly relevant to sourcing work.

quick_facts.label: Qualification pattern
quick_facts.value: Employer requirements vary: one current apparel-sourcing posting asks for a bachelor’s degree in Fashion Technology, Apparel, or Textiles plus relevant sourcing experience, while another sourcing posting accepts a degree or diploma in Fashion Technology, Textile Design, Accessories Design, Apparel Production, or a related field.

quick_facts.label: Work context
quick_facts.value: The role commonly sits between design/buying/technical teams and Indian vendors or factories; factory capability, quality, capacity, lead time, and cost are recurring operating concerns.

sections.heading: What an Apparel Sourcing Executive does
paragraphs: An Apparel Sourcing Executive manages the operational path from a product requirement to production-ready supply. Typical work includes developing and onboarding vendors; identifying factories with the necessary category capability; negotiating cost, minimum order quantities (MOQs), lead times, and payment terms; and maintaining supplier relationships.

The role also translates product needs into material and production actions: sourcing fabrics, developing trims, selecting washes or finishes where relevant, building costings from prototype through bulk, and proposing material or construction alternatives that protect quality while improving cost or feasibility.

Execution continues through sample stages and bulk production. Sourcing executives may track prototype, fit, pre-production and other sample timelines, coordinate with technical and quality teams for inspections, monitor production progress, and address supplier, quality, or delivery issues before launch or shipment.
bullets: Evaluate vendors and factories against quality, capacity, cost, and category-fit requirements.
Source fabrics, trims, accessories, and related inputs; maintain approvals and development follow-up.
Negotiate commercial terms and use costing to support margin or target-price decisions.
Coordinate sampling, production handover, quality checks, and on-time delivery with internal teams and suppliers.

sections.heading: Entry routes from the listed education pathways
paragraphs: B.F.Tech is the most directly aligned parent pathway. NIFT’s B.F.Tech (Apparel Production) curriculum includes apparel production technology, production planning, fashion marketing, sourcing and costing; it also identifies careers such as production merchandiser, vendor evaluation manager, supply-chain manager, and sourcing head. For NIFT admission, B.F.Tech applicants need a recognised 10+2 qualification with Physics and Mathematics; requirements can differ at other institutions and by admission year.

B.Sc. in Fashion Design and Diploma in Fashion Design can support entry when paired with demonstrable knowledge of garment construction, fabrics, trims, product development, costing, and supplier coordination. This is a role-specific rather than universally regulated entry standard: a current employer posting requests a bachelor’s degree in Fashion Technology/Apparel/Textiles and 2–4 years of relevant sourcing experience, while another sourcing posting lists a relevant degree or diploma among acceptable qualifications.

A practical early-career route is to gain supplier-facing experience through apparel merchandising, sampling or production coordination, quality, procurement, or material sourcing, then move into an executive sourcing remit. This reflects the overlap between sourcing work and apparel production/merchandising described in NIFT’s curriculum and employer role descriptions.
bullets: Build a portfolio or work record showing fabric and trim selection, tech-pack interpretation, costing, sample follow-up, and vendor communication.
Seek internships or junior roles with apparel brands, buying houses, export houses, manufacturers, or sourcing offices.
For B.F.Tech applicants to NIFT specifically, verify the admission-year prospectus because eligibility, age limits, seats, and selection rules are admission-cycle dependent.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: Material and product fluency is central: employers seek knowledge of fabric sourcing and costing, knit/denim or other category-specific construction, trims, washes and finishes, quality expectations, and how material or construction choices affect cost, appearance, and manufacturability.

Commercial and supplier-management skills are equally important. The executive must compare supplier capability and performance, negotiate prices and terms, manage MOQs and lead times, and maintain reliable vendor partnerships. Costing, target-price awareness, and the ability to assess capacity and quality trade-offs are recurrent requirements.

The role requires disciplined coordination: working with design, merchandising, technical, planning, warehouse, finance, quality-control, and factory teams; tracking samples and production; and using data tools such as spreadsheets for production or vendor-performance tracking. Clear communication, escalation, and problem-solving are necessary when quality or delivery diverges from plan.
bullets: Fabrics, trims, garment construction, fit, finishing, and quality inspection fundamentals.
Supplier discovery, evaluation, negotiation, and vendor relationship management.
Costing, MOQ, lead-time, capacity, and delivery-risk analysis.
Sample and production-calendar tracking; cross-functional coordination.
Spreadsheet-based tracking and data-informed decisions on costs, margins, and vendor performance.

sections.heading: Where and how the work is performed
paragraphs: Employment can be with fashion brands, e-commerce businesses, buying or sourcing offices, export houses, and apparel manufacturers. The work combines desk-based commercial and calendar management with frequent interaction with vendors and manufacturing units; the exact balance depends on the category, geography, and company operating model.

A sourcing executive commonly works cross-functionally with design, buying or merchandising, technical/product teams, planning, quality control, warehouse, and finance. Factory-side responsibilities may include capability assessment, capacity monitoring, fabric approval, production-flow compliance, and corrective action on quality deviations.
The pace is commonly calendar-driven. In e-commerce and fast-fashion settings, roles may emphasise short lead times, low MOQs, repeat styles, replenishment, and delivery against launch calendars.
bullets: Office or hybrid coordination with suppliers, factories, and internal product teams.
Factory and vendor visits may be required for capability, sample, quality, and production follow-up.
Workload often peaks around seasonal development, sample approvals, bulk-order placement, and delivery milestones.

sections.heading: Typical progression
paragraphs: Progression is commonly based on expanding ownership: from assisting with supplier follow-up, samples, materials, or a product category to independently managing vendors, commercial negotiations, production execution, and a broader sourcing portfolio. Current job listings distinguish executive/associate work from sourcing-manager and sourcing-lead roles, suggesting a pathway toward management as commercial and factory-accountability scope grows.

For graduates of NIFT’s B.F.Tech pathway, the institute identifies senior-adjacent outcomes including vendor evaluation manager, supply-chain manager, product development head, and sourcing head. These are possible directions rather than automatic promotions; advancement depends on demonstrated supplier-network strength, category expertise, quality and delivery outcomes, commercial judgment, and leadership responsibility.
bullets: Sourcing/production/merchandising assistant or coordinator → sourcing executive or associate.
Executive → category, fabric, trims, or product-development specialist; or sourcing/production manager.
Experienced professionals may progress toward vendor evaluation, supply chain, product development, or sourcing-head responsibilities.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Pay is highly variable by location, product category, employer type, experience, vendor network, travel expectations, and responsibility for costing or bulk production. It should therefore be assessed from contemporaneous job offers rather than treated as a fixed national salary.

As a dated example observed during this review, one remote India job advertisement for an experienced Apparel Sourcing Executive stated ₹30,000–₹40,000 per month and preferred 3+ years of sourcing, merchandising, or factory-management experience. This is a single employer listing, not a market-wide benchmark, and may have changed or expired after review.
bullets: Compare compensation alongside responsibility for vendor development, travel, category scope, sampling, bulk-production ownership, and sourcing-volume targets.
Confirm whether quoted pay is fixed monthly salary, total annual compensation, or a range subject to experience and location.
